instrument: Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria 1999 (as amended)
title: Right to personal liberty
text: Every person has the right to personal liberty. No person shall be deprived of his liberty except in accordance with a procedure permitted by law. A person arrested or detained shall be informed in writing within twenty-four hours of the facts and grounds for his arrest or detention.

instrument: Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria 1999 (as amended)
title: Right to fair hearing
text: In the determination of his civil rights and obligations, including any question or determination by or against any government or authority, a person shall be entitled to a fair hearing within a reasonable time by a court or other tribunal established by law.

instrument: Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria 1999 (as amended)
title: Right to acquire and own immovable property
text: Subject to the provisions of this Constitution, every citizen of Nigeria shall have the right to acquire and own immovable property anywhere in Nigeria.

instrument: Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria 1999 (as amended)
title: Compulsory acquisition and privacy
text: No moveable property or any interest in an immovable property shall be taken possession of compulsorily and no right over or interest in any such property shall be acquired compulsorily in any part of Nigeria except under a law that makes provision for compensation and for a right of access to the court. Nothing in subsection (1) shall invalidate any law that is reasonably justifiable in a democratic society for the purpose of protecting public safety, public order, public morality or public health.

title: Duties of the Police
text: The Police shall prevent and detect crime, preserve law and order, protect life and property, and carry out such other duties as may be imposed by law, with accountability to the public and respect for human rights.

title: Search without warrant — conditions
text: A police officer may search any person or premises without a warrant where he has reasonable grounds to suspect that an offence has been committed and that evidence may be found. The search must be conducted with decency and respect for personal dignity. A search of a person should be conducted by an officer of the same sex where practicable.

title: Seizure of articles
text: Where a police officer finds on a person or premises any article which he reasonably suspects to be stolen, unlawfully obtained, or evidence of an offence, he may seize it and must provide a receipt. Seizure must be proportionate to the suspected offence.

title: Search of phones and digital devices
text: A suspect's phone or digital device shall not be searched without a warrant except where the owner consents, or in exigent circumstances where delay would result in loss of evidence relating to a serious offence. Officers should document the basis for any search.

title: Arrest without warrant
text: A police officer may arrest without warrant any person who commits an offence in his presence, or whom he reasonably suspects of having committed a felony, provided the arrest is necessary. The person must be informed of the reason for arrest promptly.

title: Privacy of communications
text: Any person who intentionally intercepts or discloses private communications without lawful authority commits an offence. Coerced disclosure of passwords or device access may engage criminal and civil liability depending on circumstances.

title: Vesting of land in the Governor
text: All land comprised in the territory of each State (except land vested in the Federal Government) is vested in the Governor of that State, who holds it in trust for the people. Rights of occupancy are granted under State law.

title: Consent to transfer of rights of occupancy
text: It shall not be lawful for the holder of a statutory right of occupancy to alienate his right without the prior consent of the Governor (or appropriate local authority). Purchasers should verify that consent was obtained for any transfer.

title: Due diligence before land purchase
text: Before buying land, conduct searches at the State Land Registry and Probate Registry, verify survey plan and root of title, confirm vendor identity, check for encumbrances and litigation, and use a qualified lawyer to draft or review the deed of assignment and obtain Governor's consent where required.

title: Public documents
text: Documents forming part of public records may be proved by certified copies. Private agreements (e.g. agreements of sale) require proper execution, witnesses, and supporting title documents to be admissible and enforceable.

title: Breach of contract and remedies
text: Where a party to a contract breaches a material term (e.g. unlawful rent increase contrary to agreement, or failure to provide quiet enjoyment), the aggrieved party may seek damages, specific performance, or injunction, and should document notices and communications in writing.

title: Rent increases and eviction
text: Rent increases generally require agreement or compliance with notice periods in the tenancy agreement or applicable State law. Self-help eviction (changing locks, removing belongings without court order) is typically unlawful; landlords usually must obtain a court order for possession.

title: Land matters in Kwara
text: Land transactions in Kwara State are processed through the Ministry of Housing and Urban Development / Lands Bureau. Purchasers should conduct registry searches in Ilorin, verify Certificate of Occupancy or customary rights, and confirm survey alignment with State records.

title: Tenancy disputes in Kwara
text: Tenants in Kwara may seek redress through the Multi-Door Courthouse, Magistrate or High Court depending on claim value, and may report harassment to the Nigeria Police Force or civil authorities. Document all rent payments and notices.

title: Police misconduct complaints
text: Complaints against police conduct in Kwara may be lodged with the Police Public Relations Officer, the Kwara State Police Command, the Police Service Commission, or the National Human Rights Commission. Preserve video, audio, and witness details.

title: Functions of the Commission
text: The Commission shall monitor and investigate allegations of human rights violations, assist victims, and make recommendations to appropriate authorities including prosecution where warranted.

title: Access to legal aid
text: Indigent persons may apply to the Legal Aid Council of Nigeria for free legal assistance in criminal defence and certain civil matters including tenancy and family disputes, subject to means testing.

title: Common land fraud indicators
text: Warning signs include: pressure to pay cash without receipt, refusal to show original title documents, duplicate sales of same plot, mismatched survey plans, 'family land' without clear consent of all stakeholders, and inability to conduct registry search.

title: Formal demand before litigation
text: A written demand letter should state the parties, tenancy terms breached, facts of the breach (e.g. unlawful rent hike), legal basis, remedy sought, and a reasonable deadline (often 7–14 days) before court action. Keep copies and proof of delivery.
